A Practical Guide to Distributed Tracing

By Sam Whitley · June 20, 2026 · 12 min read · Part 1 of 4

Distributed tracing is the discipline of recording what a request did as it crossed process boundaries. When a single user click fans out across a frontend, an API gateway, a half-dozen microservices, a cache, and a queue worker, the question "why was this slow?" stops having a single answer. A trace is the artifact that gives you one.

This is the first instalment of a four-part guide. In this part we establish the vocabulary — spans, trace context, sampling — so the later parts, which cover instrumentation, propagation, and analysis, have precise words to build on. By the end you should be able to read a flame graph and explain to a colleague what a span actually is.

What is a span?

A span is a unit of work: a logical operation with a start time, a duration, and a name. Spans are nested: the root span covers the whole request, its children cover phases of the request, and so on down to individual database calls. The nesting is what reconstructs the call tree after the fact.

Each span carries a small set of attributes — the service that produced it, the operation name, a bag of free-form key/value tags, and a pair of identifiers: its own span id and the id of its parent. These identifiers are how the collector reassembles a scattered set of spans back into a tree.

Trace context

For spans to nest across processes, the trace identity has to travel with the request. That is what trace context is: two headers — a trace id and the current span id — that the caller injects and the callee extracts, using the latter as its parent id. W3C Trace Context (the traceparent header) is the standardized form; earlier formats like B3 are still common and most libraries accept both.

Propagation is the unglamorous heart of tracing. Get it wrong — drop a header on one hop, mutate the trace id mid-request, fail to extract before creating a child — and the trace silently fragments into disconnected pieces that no tooling will stitch back together. Get it right and a single request's path through a dozen services becomes one continuous tree.

Sampling

Recording every span of every request is rarely affordable at scale. Sampling decides which requests get fully traced. The two common strategies are head-based (the entry service rolls the dice once and the decision propagates) and tail-based (a collector that sees the full trace decides whether to keep it based on latency or error). Head-based is cheap and uniform; tail-based is expressive but requires buffering the whole trace in a collector.

Most production setups use a small head-based sample rate plus a tail-based layer that keeps every error and every slow request regardless of the head decision. The combination gives you representative traffic for capacity planning and complete coverage for the requests you actually want to debug.

What's next

In Part 2 we instrument a small Node service by hand — adding spans around HTTP handlers, database queries, and outbound calls — and then show the same service auto-instrumented with OpenTelemetry, so you can see exactly what the library is doing for you.
